docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Install Nyra
docs.body: Install Nyra Install the Nyra toolchain from a GitHub release tag , set up your environment, and run your first project on macOS , Linux , or Windows . Repository: github.com/nyra-lang/nyra Releases (tags): github.com/nyra-lang/nyra/releases Public repository required for the one-line installer The repo must be public (or you must be logged in) to download release assets and to use the curl | sh installer. Private repos require manual download from the Releases page while signed in, or building from source. On this page 1. Requirements 2. Pick a release tag 3. Install layout 4. macOS 5. Linux 6. Windows 7. First project 8. Upgrade 9. Build from source 10. Uninstall 11. Troubleshooting Quick reference

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 1. Requirements (all platforms)
docs.body: 1. Requirements (all platforms) Tool Why clang Links your .ny programs with the Nyra C runtime tar Extract release archives (macOS/Linux)

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: clang install hints
docs.body: clang install hints OS Install macOS xcode-select --install (Command Line Tools) Linux (Debian/Ubuntu) sudo apt update && sudo apt install clang Linux (Fedora) sudo dnf install clang Windows (native) LLVM for Windows — add clang to PATH , or use WSL2 (recommended in §6 )

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Cross-compilation toolchains (optional)
docs.body: Cross-compilation toolchains (optional) To build for another OS (e.g. release a Windows .exe from macOS), install a cross linker/sysroot in addition to clang : Goal Typical setup macOS → Linux brew install messense/macos-cross-toolchains/aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu (or x86_64 variant) macOS / Linux → Windows MinGW-w64 + clang ( brew install mingw-w64 on macOS) → macOS from Linux/Windows osxcross + Apple SDK (advanced) → Wasm Homebrew llvm (Apple clang lacks wasm32-wasi ) Then: nyra build . --release --for windows . See Compilation targets .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 2. Pick a release tag
docs.body: 2. Pick a release tag Open Releases . Choose a version (e.g. v0.0.1 ). Under Assets , download the file that matches your CPU and OS (see table). Your machine Release asset Mac, Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) nyra-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz Mac, Intel nyra-x86_64-darwin.tar.gz Linux, 64-bit Intel/AMD nyra-x86_64-linux.tar.gz Linux, 64-bit ARM (e.g. Raspberry Pi 4/5, ARM cloud) nyra-aarch64-linux.tar.gz Windows (native) No prebuilt yet — use Windows below Optional: verify checksums using SHA256SUMS on the same release page.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 3. Install layout
docs.body: 3. Install layout After extracting, you should have: ~/.nyra/ (recommended; or any folder you choose) bin/nyra share/stdlib/nyra_rt.c version # e.g. 1.0.0 Set these environment variables (add to your shell profile so they persist): export NYRA_HOME="$HOME/.nyra" export PATH="$NYRA_HOME/bin:$PATH"

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 4. macOS
docs.body: 4. macOS

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — One-line installer (easiest)
docs.body: Option A — One-line installer (easiest) Requires a public repo and curl : curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh Pin a specific tag: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 Reload shell: source ~/.zshrc # or ~/.bashrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option B — Manual install from a release tag
docs.body: Option B — Manual install from a release tag Download nyra-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz or nyra-x86_64-darwin.tar.gz from Releases . Extract: mkdir -p ~/.nyra tar -xzf ~/Downloads/nyra-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz -C ~/.nyra Add to ~/.zshrc (or ~/.bash_profile ): export NYRA_HOME="$HOME/.nyra" export PATH="$NYRA_HOME/bin:$PATH" If macOS blocks the binary ( killed on first run): xattr -cr ~/.nyra/bin/nyra Verify: source ~/.zshrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 5. Linux
docs.body: 5. Linux

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — One-line installer
docs.body: Option A — One-line installer curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh # or pin version: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 source ~/.bashrc # or ~/.zshrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 6. Windows
docs.body: 6. Windows Prebuilt Windows binaries ship on every release tag as nyra-x86_64-windows.zip .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — PowerShell installer (recommended)
docs.body: Option A — PowerShell installer (recommended) irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.ps1 | iex # or pin version: .\scripts\install.ps1 -Version 1.1.0 nyra --version Requires clang (LLVM for Windows) on PATH to link .ny programs.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option B — Manual zip from Releases
docs.body: Option B — Manual zip from Releases Download nyra-x86_64-windows.zip from Releases . Extract to e.g. %USERPROFILE%\.nyra . Add %USERPROFILE%\.nyra\bin to PATH and set NYRA_HOME . Dot-source env.ps1 in the zip or open a new terminal after the installer updated user PATH.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option C — WSL2 (Ubuntu)
docs.body: Option C — WSL2 (Ubuntu) Install WSL2 and Ubuntu. Inside WSL, install clang: sudo apt install clang curl tar . Follow Linux (§5) — installer or manual tarball nyra-x86_64-linux.tar.gz . Create projects under your Linux home (e.g. ~/projects/myapp ).

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Alternative — Build from source (native Windows)
docs.body: Alternative — Build from source (native Windows) Install Rust and clang (LLVM) on Windows. Clone the repo (same tag you want, e.g. v0.0.1 ): git clone https://github.com/nyra-lang/nyra.git cd nyra git checkout v0.0.1 cargo build --release -p nyra-cli Use the built CLI and point runtime at the repo stdlib : $env:PATH = "$PWD\target\release;$env:PATH" $env:NYRA_HOME = "$PWD" .\target\release\nyra.exe --version Run examples from the repo root: .\target\release\nyra.exe run examples\syntax\hello.ny Native Windows support is evolving; WSL2 matches the Linux release workflow today.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Install nyrapkg (package manager)
docs.body: Install nyrapkg (package manager) Dependency management uses the standalone nyrapkg tool ( guide · github.com/nyra-lang/pkg ), installed alongside nyra under ~/.nyra/bin/ : curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/pkg/main/scripts/install.sh | sh nyrapkg --version From source: nyra build --release -o nyrapkg . in the pkg repo, then nyrapkg bootstrap .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 7. First project (all platforms)
docs.body: 7. First project (all platforms) Once nyra --version works (e.g. nyra 0.0.1 ): mkdir myapp cd myapp nyrapkg init This creates: nyra.mod , nyra.lock , nyra.sum main.ny with a Hello, world program Run it: nyra run main.ny Expected output: Hello, world! Multi-file projects For apps with import , run from the project root: nyra run . — see Modules and Getting started . Other useful commands: nyra check nyra build nyra build --release nyra fmt --write main.ny

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 8. Upgrade to a newer tag
docs.body: 8. Upgrade to a newer tag Open Releases and pick a newer tag (e.g. v0.1.2 ). macOS/Linux: re-run the install script with --version , or download the new .tar.gz and extract over ~/.nyra . Confirm: nyra --version .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 9. Build from source (contributors)
docs.body: 9. Build from source (contributors) If you work on the compiler itself: git clone https://github.com/nyra-lang/nyra.git cd nyra cargo build --release -p nyra-cli export PATH="$PWD/target/release:$PATH" nyra run examples/syntax/hello.ny Or: cargo install --path cli Local iteration: ./scripts/dev-install.sh from the repo root.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 10. Uninstall
docs.body: 10. Uninstall rm -rf ~/.nyra Remove the NYRA_HOME / PATH lines from ~/.zshrc , ~/.bashrc , or ~/.bash_profile .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 11. Troubleshooting
docs.body: 11. Troubleshooting Problem Fix clang not found Install clang (see §1 ) release asset not found Tag not published yet, wrong arch asset, or private repo without login Runtime not found Set export NYRA_HOME=~/.nyra and ensure share/stdlib/nyra_rt.c exists zsh: killed (macOS) xattr -cr ~/.nyra/bin/nyra Failed to read main.ny Run nyrapkg init first, or create main.ny 404 on raw.githubusercontent.com Repo is private — use Releases download or make repo public

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Quick reference
docs.body: Quick reference Step Command Latest install (mac/Linux, public repo) curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh Pin version ... | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 Package manager curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/pkg/main/scripts/install.sh | sh New project nyrapkg init && nyra run . Releases page github.com/nyra-lang/nyra/releases Canonical source: install.md in the repository. Getting started → · Overview

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Nyra Keywords
docs.body: Nyra Keywords Every reserved word and type name the lexer treats as a keyword — plus how to reuse those names as variables with a leading @ , and how compiler attributes differ ( #[…] , not @ ). On this page @ vs #[ ] Raw identifiers ( @name ) Reserved words list Bindings & constants Control flow Types, modules & functions Memory & ownership Concurrency & async Type names Compiler attributes ( #[…] ) Quick reference

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: @ vs #[ ]
docs.body: @ vs #[ ] Nyra uses two different prefix forms. They are not interchangeable. Syntax Role Example @name Raw identifier — use a reserved word or type name as a variable / field name let @module = 10 #[attr] Compiler attribute — metadata on functions, structs, or parameters #[derive(Copy)] module … Keyword — declare a module path module my.app clone x Keyword — explicit heap duplicate at a call site save(clone user) @module and @clone in source are not special builtins — they are ordinary identifiers whose names happen to match keywords. See Raw identifiers below.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Raw identifiers ( @name )
docs.body: Raw identifiers ( @name ) When a name is reserved (keyword or built-in type), you cannot use it bare as an identifier. Prefix it with @ — the same idea as Rust r#name . The lexer strips the @ and treats the token as a normal Identifier . The compiler stores the name without the prefix. Without types With types fn main() { let @module = 10 let @clone = @module print(@clone) // prints 10 } fn main() -> void { let @module = 10 let @clone = @module print(@clone) // prints 10 } Here @module is a variable named module ; @clone is a variable named clone . This is unrelated to the module declaration keyword or the clone ownership prefix.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: When @ is required
docs.body: When @ is required Any name that the lexer would otherwise parse as a keyword or type token needs @ to be used as an identifier: Without @ With @ module → module keyword @module → variable named module clone → clone prefix @clone → variable named clone fn → function keyword @fn → variable named fn i32 → type i32 @i32 → variable named i32

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Rules
docs.body: Rules @ must be followed by a letter or _ (not a digit). let x = @ alone is a lexer error ( Expected identifier after '@' ). let x = @1 is invalid for the same reason. C bindings from nyra pkg c add may append _ to reserved C names instead of using @ (see C Bindgen ).

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Reserved words list
docs.body: Reserved words list The table below matches the compiler’s reserved-name list used by C bindgen ( c-bindgen/src/names.rs ). These names cannot appear as bare identifiers in Nyra source. let mut fn if else while return true false print import module struct impl self for const extern export inst enum match spawn in test async await trait macro defer unsafe asm as move clone void bool string ptr char type out i8 i16 i32 i64 i128 u8 u16 u32 u64 u128 isize usize f64 The lexer also reserves additional tokens (e.g. break , continue , pub , priv , dyn , f32 , benchmark , parallel , progress ). Treat any keyword from the language reference as requiring @ when used as an identifier.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Bindings & constants
docs.body: Bindings & constants Keyword Purpose let Immutable binding — value cannot be reassigned mut Used with let mut for a reassignable binding const Compile-time constant name true / false Boolean literals

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Control flow
docs.body: Control flow Keyword Purpose if / else Conditional branches while Loop while condition is true for / in Iteration — for i in 0..10 or for x in arr match Pattern match on enums and values return Return from the current function

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Types, modules & functions
docs.body: Types, modules & functions Keyword Purpose fn Function definition struct Product type with named fields enum Sum type with variants impl Methods on a type; impl Trait for Type trait Trait definition (Extended) self Method receiver inside impl import Load another .ny file module Module path declaration — see Modules extern Declare an external (C) function export Export a symbol for FFI inst Generic instance / monomorph hook (tooling) type Type alias or associated type surface macro Macro definition (Extended) test Test function — run with nyra test print Built-in stdout; optional color: for ANSI output as Type cast — expr as Type unsafe Block for raw pointers and low-level operations asm Inline assembly (inside unsafe ) defer Scope-exit call (LIFO) — Extended; prefer impl Drop out Output / FFI direction marker in bindings

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Memory & ownership
docs.body: Memory & ownership Keyword Purpose move Unary prefix at a call site — transfer ownership explicitly ( guide ) clone Unary prefix or .clone() — heap duplicate; original stays valid clone user and let @clone = user mean completely different things: the first duplicates a value; the second binds a variable whose name is the word clone .

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Concurrency & async
docs.body: Concurrency & async Keyword Purpose spawn Start a concurrent block async / await Async functions and suspension (Extended)

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Type names
docs.body: Type names These tokens are parsed as type names, not ordinary identifiers. Use @ if you need a variable with the same spelling. Type Notes i8 … i128 Signed integers; i32 is the default for int literals u8 … u128 Unsigned integers isize , usize Pointer-sized integers f64 IEEE-754 double; default float literal type char Unicode scalar — 'a' , '\n' bool true / false string UTF-8 heap string (Move) void Functions with no return value ptr Opaque FFI pointer handle Full type reference: Types & data · Language reference → Type names

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Compiler attributes ( #[…] )
docs.body: Compiler attributes ( #[…] ) Attributes attach to items in source. They start with # , not @ . Attribute Purpose #[derive(Copy)] Document / validate that a struct is Copy-safe #[derive(Clone)] Synthesize .clone() when fields allow it #[comptime] Evaluate this function at compile time; strip from binary — Comptime guide #[inline] Hint inlining at call sites #[hot] / #[cold] LLVM branch-hint attributes for hot / cold paths #[no_escape] On &T parameters — value must not escape the function — Escape analysis Without types With types #[derive(Copy)] struct Point { x: i32 y: i32 } #[comptime] fn table_size() { return 256 } fn process(#[no_escape] data) { ... } #[derive(Copy)] struct Point { x: i32 y: i32 } #[comptime] fn table_size() -> i32 { return 256 } fn process(#[no_escape] data: i32) { ... }

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Quick reference
docs.body: Quick reference You write Meaning @module Variable (or field) named module module my.app Declare module path my.app @clone Variable named clone clone user Duplicate user for a call; keep user valid #[derive(Copy)] Compiler attribute on a struct @i32 Variable named i32 (not type annotation)

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: LLVM @ (not Nyra source)
docs.body: LLVM @ (not Nyra source) Generated LLVM IR uses @symbol for global functions (e.g. @i32_to_string ). That is LLVM notation, not something you write in .ny files. Language reference →

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Language basics
docs.body: Language basics Short reference: variables, print, if, loops, and functions.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: let and let mut
docs.body: let and let mut let is immutable. Use let mut to change the value. Without types With types fn main() { let name = "Sam" let mut score = 0 score = score + 10 print(score) } fn main() -> void { let name: string = "Sam" let mut score: i32 = 0 score = score + 10 print(score) } Output 10

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: print
docs.body: print print writes to the terminal with a newline after each call. Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, Nyra!") print(42) } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, Nyra!") print(42) } Output Hello, Nyra! 42

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: if
docs.body: if Branch execution on a boolean condition. Without types With types fn main() { let n = 7 if n > 5 { print("big") } else { print("small") } } fn main() -> void { let n: i32 = 7 if n > 5 { print("big") } else { print("small") } } Output big

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: for and while
docs.body: for and while for over a numeric range; while while the condition is true. Without types With types fn main() { for i in 0..3 { print(i) } let mut n = 0 while n < 2 { print(n) n = n + 1 } } fn main() -> void { for i in 0..3 { print(i) } let mut n: i32 = 0 while n < 2 { print(n) n = n + 1 } } Output 0 1 2 0 1

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: fn
docs.body: fn Define a function with parameters and call it from main . Without types With types fn add(a, b) { return a + b } fn main() { print(add(2, 3)) } fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { return a + b } fn main() -> void { print(add(2, 3)) } Output 5 Syntax cheat sheet →

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Language syntax
docs.body: Language syntax Core syntax for Nyra — indentation-friendly blocks, minimal keywords. New to Nyra? Start with Language basics . See also Language reference and Standard library .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Hello World
docs.body: Hello World Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, world!") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, world!") }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Variables
docs.body: Variables New to programming? Read the full walkthrough: Language basics → Variables .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: let — immutable binding
docs.body: let — immutable binding Creates a name for a value. After initialization, you must not assign a new value to that name. Without types With types let x = 42 // x stays 42 unless you used let mut let x: i32 = 42

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: mut — mutable (changeable)
docs.body: mut — mutable (changeable) mut is short for mutable . Use let mut when the value will change (counters, loops). Without types With types let mut n = 0 // n can be reassigned n = n + 1 let mut n: i32 = 0 n = n + 1

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: const — compile-time constant
docs.body: const — compile-time constant Without types With types const MAX = 100 // fixed name; value known when compiling const MAX = 100 // fixed name; value known when compiling let let mut const Reassign? No Yes No When set? At runtime in code At runtime; can change At compile time string and heap-owned values are Move types — see Memory & ownership .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Functions
docs.body: Functions Without types With types fn add(a, b) { return a + b } fn greet(name) { print(name) } fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { return a + b } fn greet(name: string) { print(name) } Generic syntax is supported with monomorphization ( fn id<T>(x: T) ).

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Template strings
docs.body: Template strings Without types With types let name = "Nyra" let score = 42 print(`Hello, ${name}!`) print(`score) let name: string = "Nyra" let score: i32 = 42 print(`Hello, ${name}!`) print(`score)

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Arrow functions
docs.body: Arrow functions Without types With types let add_one = (x) => x + 1 print(add_one(41)) let add_one = (x) => x + 1 print(add_one(41)) Emits warning[W001] unless you use --deny-extended in CI. Useful for HTTP handler slots — see net/http .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Control flow
docs.body: Control flow

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: if / else
docs.body: if / else if x > 0 { print("positive") } else { print("non-positive") }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: while
docs.body: while Without types With types let mut i = 0 while i < 10 { print(i) i = i + 1 } let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 10 { print(i) i = i + 1 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: break / continue
docs.body: break / continue break exits the nearest loop; continue skips to the next iteration ( while and range for ). Without types With types let mut sum = 0 let mut i = 0 while i < 10 { i = i + 1 if i == 5 { continue } sum = sum + i } let mut sum: i32 = 0 let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 10 { i = i + 1 if i == 5 { continue } sum = sum + i }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: for range
docs.body: for range Without types With types for i in 0..5 { print(i) } for i in 0..5 { print(i) }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: if expressions
docs.body: if expressions Without types With types let sign = if x >= 0 { 1 } else { -1 } let sign = if x >= 0 { 1 } else { -1 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: match
docs.body: match Exhaustive-style matching on enums and values. Supports guards — see Match . Without types With types let n = match color { Color.Red if x > 0 => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } let n = match color { Color.Red if x > 0 => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Methods
docs.body: Methods Defined with impl ; desugared to Type_method functions: Without types With types impl Calculator { fn add(self, n) { Calculator { value: self.value + n } } } impl Calculator { fn add(self, n: i32) { Calculator { value: self.value + n } } } Without types With types let c2 = c.add(10) let c2 = c.add(10)

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: I/O
docs.body: I/O Without types With types print("line") // stdout + newline print(42) // integers write("no newline") // buffered println("line") // buffered + newline flush() // flush buffer let s = input("Name? ") print("line") // stdout + newline print(42) // integers write("no newline") // buffered println("line") // buffered + newline flush() // flush buffer let s: string = input("Name? ")

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Colored output
docs.body: Colored output Add an optional color: argument to print . Works in ANSI-capable terminals (macOS Terminal, iTerm, Windows Terminal). print("Error!", color: red) print("OK", color: green) print("Custom", color: "#FF5733") // #RGB or #RRGGBB print("RGB", color: "rgb(0,255,128)") print(msg, color: myColor) // string variable Named colors: red , green , blue , yellow , cyan , magenta , bold , dim , bright_red , … See I/O built-ins .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: String escapes
docs.body: String escapes Double-quoted strings support \n , \t , \\ , \" , octal ( \033 ), hex ( \x1b ), and Unicode ( \u{1b} ).

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Date & time
docs.body: Date & time Without types With types let d = date() print(d.year) print(d.month) // 1–12 print(d.day) print(d.hour) print(d.minute) print(d.second) print(d.week) // 0=Sunday … 6=Saturday let d: Date = date() print(d.year) print(d.month) // 1–12 print(d.day) print(d.hour) print(d.minute) print(d.second) print(d.week) // 0=Sunday … 6=Saturday Aliases: d.minutes , d.seconds , d.weekday . Full reference: Built-ins → date() .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: String & array built-ins
docs.body: String & array built-ins Without types With types let parts = "a,b,c".split(",") for p in parts { print(p) } let arr = [10, 20, 30] for n in arr { print(n) } print(arr.length()) let parts: VecStr = "a,b,c".split(",") for p in parts { print(p) } let arr: [i32; 3] = [10, 20, 30] for n in arr { print(n) } print(arr.length()) String methods: .trim() , .contains() , .replace() , .to_upper() , … — full list .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: defer (Extended — prefer Drop)
docs.body: defer (Extended — prefer Drop) Run a call expression when the block ends (LIFO). Extended tier — emits warning[W001] . For memory, auto-drop already frees at } ; for handles, use impl Drop RAII instead of defer free . Without types With types extern fn close_fd(fd){ let fd = open(path) defer close_fd(fd) // OK: FFI teardown; prefer impl Drop for reusable types read_into(fd) } // owned string locals: auto-dropped — no defer needed extern fn close_fd(fd){ let fd = open(path) defer close_fd(fd) // OK: FFI teardown; prefer impl Drop for reusable types read_into(fd) } // owned string locals: auto-dropped — no defer needed Details: Memory → defer vs Drop · nyra-skill.md

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Timing & memory (builtins)
docs.body: Timing & memory (builtins) No import required — same ergonomics as print and date() : time_start("loop") mem_start("RAM") // ... work ... time_end("loop") mem_end("RAM") Full API: Standard library → Built-ins .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: FFI
docs.body: FFI extern fn printf(fmt: string, ...) -> i32 export fn my_api() -> i32 { return 0 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Tests
docs.body: Tests Without types With types test fn adds() { let sum = 1 + 2 print(sum) } test fn adds() { let sum = 1 + 2 print(sum) }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Comments
docs.body: Comments Line comments with // . Block comments are planned. See Types & data for structs, enums, arrays, and references. Complete keyword/operator list: Language reference . Language reference →

docs.title: Arrays
docs.heading: Arrays
docs.body: Arrays Fixed-size arrays and indexing.

docs.title: Arrays
docs.heading: Array
docs.body: Array Index starts at 0. All elements share the same type. Without types With types fn main() { let nums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums[0]) print(nums[1]) } fn main() -> void { let nums: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3] print(nums[0]) print(nums[1]) } Output 1 2 ← Data Structures Learn overview Vectors →

docs.title: Booleans
docs.heading: Booleans
docs.body: Booleans The bool type: true and false .

docs.title: Booleans
docs.heading: bool
docs.body: bool Comparisons and logical operators produce bool values used in if and loops. Without types With types fn main() { let passed = true print(passed) print(5 > 3) } fn main() -> void { let passed = true print(passed) print(5 > 3) } Output true true ← Operators Learn overview If..Else →

docs.title: Borrowing
docs.heading: Borrowing
docs.body: Borrowing Temporary access with &T and &mut T .

docs.title: Borrowing
docs.heading: Mutable borrow
docs.body: Mutable borrow Only one mutable borrow at a time. Finish using it before returning to the owner. Without types With types fn main() { mut x = 1 let r = &mut x print(*r) unsafe { *r = 5 } print(x) } fn main() -> void { mut x = 1 let r = &mut x print(*r) unsafe { *r = 5 } print(x) } Output 1 5

docs.title: Borrowing
docs.heading: Shared read-only borrow
docs.body: Shared read-only borrow Multiple &T borrows are allowed together. Without types With types fn main() { let msg = "hello" let r = &msg print(*r) print(msg) } fn main() -> void { let msg: string = "hello" let r = &msg print(*r) print(msg) } Output hello hello ← Ownership Learn overview Data Structures →

docs.title: Comments
docs.heading: Comments
docs.body: Comments Comment code with // or /* */ .

docs.title: Comments
docs.heading: Line comments
docs.body: Line comments Everything after // is ignored until end of line. Without types With types // this is a comment let x = 1 // after code print(x) // this is a comment let x: i32 = 1 print(x) Output 1

docs.title: Comments
docs.heading: Block comments
docs.body: Block comments /* ... */ spans multiple lines (non-nested). Without types With types /* * file header */ let total = 1 /* mid-line */ + 2 print(total) /* * file header */ let total = 1 /* mid-line */ + 2 print(total) Output 3 ← Output Learn overview Variables →

docs.title: Constants
docs.heading: Constants
docs.body: Constants Compile-time values with const .

docs.title: Constants
docs.heading: const
docs.body: const const cannot be reassigned and is evaluated at compile time. Unlike let , which creates a runtime binding. Without types With types const MAX_LIVES = 3 fn main() { print(MAX_LIVES) } const MAX_LIVES = 3 fn main() -> void { print(MAX_LIVES) } Output 3 ← Data Types Learn overview Operators →

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.heading: Data Structures
docs.body: Data Structures Arrays, vectors, tuples, maps, structs, enums.

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.heading: Topic guide
docs.body: Topic guide Pick a topic: Arrays — fixed size Vectors — growable Tuples — grouped values HashMap — key → value Structs — named fields Enums — fixed variants

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.heading: Quick tour
docs.body: Quick tour Array + tuple + HashMap in one example. Without types With types import "stdlib/map" fn main() { let nums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums.len()) let pair = (42, "ok") print(pair.0) let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) } import "stdlib/map" fn main() -> void { let nums: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3] print(nums.len()) let pair = (42, "ok") print(pair.0) let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) } Output 3 42 100 ← Borrowing Learn overview Arrays →

docs.title: Data Types
docs.heading: Data Types
docs.body: Data Types Core built-in types.

docs.title: Data Types
docs.heading: Quick reference
docs.body: Quick reference Most-used built-in types: Type Example Notes i32 42 32-bit signed integer (default for literals) i64 10000000000 64-bit signed integer bool true Boolean string "hi" UTF-8 text (heap-owned when dynamic) void — No return value

docs.title: Data Types
docs.heading: Examples by type
docs.body: Examples by type You can write the type explicitly or let the compiler infer it. Without types With types fn main() { let count = 42 let flag = true let name = "Nyra" print(count) print(flag) print(name) } fn main() -> void { let count: i32 = 42 let flag = true let name: string = "Nyra" print(count) print(flag) print(name) } Output 42 true Nyra ← Variables Learn overview Constants →

docs.title: Enums
docs.heading: Enums
docs.body: Enums Named variants with match .

docs.title: Enums
docs.heading: enum + match
docs.body: enum + match In v0.2 enums are tag-only (no payloads on variants). Without types With types enum Status { Ok Err Pending } fn main() { let s = Status.Ok let code = match s { Status.Ok => 0 Status.Err => 1 Status.Pending => 2 } print(code) } enum Status { Ok Err Pending } fn main() -> void { let s = Status.Ok let code = match s { Status.Ok => 0 Status.Err => 1 Status.Pending => 2 } print(code) } Output 0 ← Structs Learn overview

docs.title: For
docs.heading: for .. in ..
docs.body: for .. in .. 0..3 means from 0 up to (but not including) 3. Without types With types fn main() { for j in 0..3 { print(j) } } fn main() -> void { for j in 0..3 { print(j) } } Output 0 1 2 ← While Learn overview Functions →

docs.title: Functions
docs.heading: Functions
docs.body: Functions Define reusable functions.

docs.title: Functions
docs.heading: fn
docs.body: fn Parameters and return types are optional when inferred; here they are explicit for learning. Without types With types fn double(n){ return n + n } fn main() { print(double(5)) } fn double(n: i32){ return n + n } fn main() -> void { print(double(5)) } Output 10

docs.title: Functions
docs.heading: Shorthand body
docs.body: Shorthand body Single-expression function with = . Without types With types fn square(n)= n * n fn main() { print(square(4)) } fn square(n: i32)= n * n fn main() -> void { print(square(4)) } Output 16 ← For Learn overview Scope →

docs.title: Get Started
docs.heading: Quick start

docs.title: Get Started

docs.body: HashMap Key → value maps via stdlib.

docs.title: HashMap
docs.heading: HashMap_str_i32
docs.body: HashMap_str_i32 Import stdlib/map then use insert / get . Without types With types import "stdlib/map" fn main() { let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("score", 100) print(scores.get("score")) } import "stdlib/map" fn main() -> void { let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("score", 100) print(scores.get("score")) } Output 100 ← Tuples Learn overview Structs →

docs.title: If..Else
docs.heading: If..Else
docs.body: If..Else Branch on a condition.

docs.title: If..Else
docs.heading: if / else
docs.body: if / else Run one block when the condition is true, otherwise the other. Without types With types fn main() { let score = 75 if score >= 60 { print("Pass") } else { print("Fail") } } fn main() -> void { let score: i32 = 75 if score >= 60 { print("Pass") } else { print("Fail") } } Output Pass

docs.title: If..Else
docs.heading: if as expression
docs.body: if as expression if can return a value directly. Without types With types fn main() { let score = 95 let label = if score >= 90 { "A" } else { "B" } print(label) } fn main() -> void { let score: i32 = 95 let label = if score >= 90 { "A" } else { "B" } print(label) } Output A ← Booleans Learn overview Match →

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: Nyra Intro
docs.body: Nyra Intro What Nyra is and how to read this track.

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: Your first program
docs.body: Your first program Nyra is a fast, memory-safe programming language with Rust-inspired ownership, a minimal syntax, and LLVM-backed native compilation. Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, Nyra!") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, Nyra!") } Output Hello, Nyra!

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: What you will learn
docs.body: What you will learn This tutorial follows a W3Schools-style path: one topic per page, runnable examples, and prev/next navigation. Start with Get Started after Install Nyra . Learn overview Get Started →

docs.title: Loops
docs.heading: Loops
docs.body: Loops Overview of while and for .

docs.title: Loops
docs.heading: Sum with for
docs.body: Sum with for while repeats while the condition is true. for i in a..b iterates a half-open range. Without types With types fn main() { let mut sum = 0 for i in 0..5 { sum = sum + i } print(sum) } fn main() -> void { let mut sum: i32 = 0 for i in 0..5 { sum = sum + i } print(sum) } Output 10 ← Match Learn overview While →

docs.title: Match
docs.heading: match
docs.body: match Each arm covers one case. Use _ for the default. Without types With types enum Color { Red Green Blue } fn main() { let c = Color.Red let n = match c { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } print(n) } enum Color { Red Green Blue } fn main() -> void { let c = Color.Red let n = match c { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } print(n) } Output 1 ← If..Else Learn overview Loops →

docs.title: Operators
docs.heading: Operators
docs.body: Operators Arithmetic, comparison, and logic.

docs.title: Operators
docs.heading: Operators
docs.body: Operators + - * / % arithmetic · == != < > compare · && || ! logic.

docs.title: Operators
docs.heading: Example
docs.body: Example Operation results print as-is. Without types With types fn main() { print(10 + 5) print(10 == 5) } fn main() -> void { print(10 + 5) print(10 == 5) } Output 15 false ← Constants Learn overview Booleans →

docs.title: Output
docs.heading: Output
docs.body: Output Print text or numbers with print .

docs.title: Output
docs.heading: print
docs.body: print print writes to the terminal and adds a newline after each call. Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, Nyra!") print(42) } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, Nyra!") print(42) } Output Hello, Nyra! 42 ← Nyra Syntax Learn overview Comments →

docs.title: Ownership
docs.heading: Quick rule
docs.body: Quick rule i32 and bool are Copied . Heap string values are Moved — the previous owner becomes invalid.

docs.title: Ownership
docs.heading: Move example
docs.body: Move example After let b = a on an owned string, do not use a . Without types With types fn main() { let a = "hello" let b = a print(b) } fn main() -> void { let a: string = "hello" let b = a print(b) } Output hello ← Strings Learn overview Borrowing →

docs.title: Scope
docs.heading: Scope
docs.body: Scope Where a variable is visible.

docs.title: Scope
docs.heading: Block scope
docs.body: Block scope Each { } block creates a new scope. Bindings declared inside a block are not visible outside it. Without types With types fn main() { let x = 1 if true { let y = 2 print(x) print(y) } } fn main() -> void { let x: i32 = 1 if true { let y: i32 = 2 print(x) print(y) } } Output 1 2 ← Functions Learn overview Strings →

docs.title: Strings
docs.heading: Strings
docs.body: Strings Strings, templates, and printing.

docs.title: Strings
docs.heading: String literal
docs.body: String literal Text between double quotes. Without types With types fn main() { let greeting = "Hello" print(greeting) } fn main() -> void { let greeting: string = "Hello" print(greeting) } Output Hello

docs.title: Strings
docs.heading: Template strings
docs.body: Template strings Use backticks with {name} to embed values. Without types With types fn main() { let name = "Nyra" print(`Hello, {name}!`) } fn main() -> void { let name: string = "Nyra" print(`Hello, {name}!`) } Output Hello, Nyra! ← Scope Learn overview Ownership →

docs.title: Structs
docs.heading: Structs
docs.body: Structs Custom types with named fields.

docs.title: Structs
docs.heading: struct
docs.body: struct Define the shape then create a value with fields. Without types With types fn main() { let p = { hp: 100, score: 0 } print(p.hp) } fn main() -> void { let p = { hp: 100, score: 0 } print(p.hp) } Output 100 ← HashMap Learn overview Enums →

docs.title: Nyra Syntax
docs.heading: Nyra Syntax
docs.body: Nyra Syntax Blocks, statements, and naming.

docs.title: Nyra Syntax
docs.heading: Program entry
docs.body: Program entry Nyra uses curly braces for blocks. Statements end at newline (no semicolons required). Function names use snake_case ; types and structs use PascalCase . Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello") } Output Hello ← Get Started Learn overview Output →

docs.title: Tuples
docs.heading: Tuples
docs.body: Tuples Group values of different types.

docs.title: Tuples
docs.heading: Tuple
docs.body: Tuple Access fields with .0 or destructure with let (a, b) = pair . Without types With types fn main() { let pair = (1, "hi") print(pair.0) let (a, b) = pair print(a) } fn main() -> void { let pair = (1, "hi") print(pair.0) let (a, b) = pair print(a) } Output 1 1 ← Vectors Learn overview HashMap →

docs.title: Variables
docs.heading: Variables
docs.body: Variables Bind a name to a value with let and let mut .

docs.title: Variables
docs.heading: let and let mut
docs.body: let and let mut let creates an immutable binding. Use let mut when the value must change. Shorthand inside functions: mut x = 0 . Without types With types fn main() { let name = "Sam" let mut score = 0 score = score + 10 print(score) } fn main() -> void { let name: string = "Sam" let mut score: i32 = 0 score = score + 10 print(score) } Output 10 ← Comments Learn overview Data Types →

docs.title: Vectors
docs.heading: Vectors
docs.body: Vectors Growable lists with vec() .

docs.title: Vectors
docs.heading: vec()
docs.body: vec() Push elements and read length and index. Higher-order helpers like filter ship. Without types With types fn main() { let xs = vec().push(10).push(20).push(30) print(xs.len()) print(xs.get(0)) } fn main() -> void { let xs = vec().push(10).push(20).push(30) print(xs.len()) print(xs.get(0)) } Output 3 10 ← Arrays Learn overview Tuples →

docs.title: While
docs.heading: While
docs.body: While Repeat while the condition is true.

docs.title: While
docs.heading: while
docs.body: while Update the variable inside the loop until the condition becomes false (or you get an infinite loop). Without types With types fn main() { let mut i = 0 while i < 3 { print(i) i = i + 1 } } fn main() -> void { let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 3 { print(i) i = i + 1 } } Output 0 1 2 ← Loops Learn overview For →

docs.title: Learning path
docs.body: Learning path Structured path from zero to a full multi-file app. Install → Get Started Learn Nyra — W3Schools-style tutorial (27 topics) Language basics → Syntax → Types → Reference Nyra Ownership → Nyra Borrowing → Ownership UX → Memory & ownership → Standard library → Built-in methods → OS, files & hardware Built-in methods → Language spec Generics → Async → FFI Learn track capstone — enums & match AI assistants: download nyra-skill.md — full syntax, types, loops, imports, and anti-hallucination rules. Getting started →

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Match expressions
docs.body: Match expressions Exhaustiveness, guards, and tag-only enums. Without types With types let n = match color { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } let n = match color { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Nested enum binds
docs.body: Nested enum binds Peel a payload enum inside a variant arm. Shorthand infers the inner enum from the payload type: Without types With types enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } enum Result_Opt { Ok(Option_i32), Fail(Option_i32) } match r { Result_Opt.Ok(Some(x)) => x Result_Opt.Ok(Option_i32.None) => 0 Result_Opt.Fail(_) => -1 } enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } enum Result_Opt { Ok(Option_i32), Fail(Option_i32) } match r { Result_Opt.Ok(Some(x)) => x Result_Opt.Ok(Option_i32.None) => 0 Result_Opt.Fail(_) => -1 } Enum variants with different payload types in one enum (e.g. Ok(Option) + Err(i32) ) are not supported yet.

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Struct and tuple patterns
docs.body: Struct and tuple patterns destructure structs and tuples in match arms. Field shorthand binds the field name; use _ to ignore a slot. Without types With types match p { Point { x, y } => x + y } match pair { (a, b) => a + b } match p { Point { x, y } => x + y } match pair { (a, b) => a + b } Field-value patterns (e.g. Point { x: 0, y } ) are not supported yet — patterns bind fields only.

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Or-patterns
docs.body: Or-patterns Combine patterns that share the same body with | (single pipe, not || ): Without types With types match c { Color.Red | Color.Blue => 1 Color.Green => 2 } match method { "GET" | "HEAD" => 200 "POST" | "PUT" => 201 _ => 400 } match c { Color.Red | Color.Blue => 1 Color.Green => 2 } match method { "GET" | "HEAD" => 200 "POST" | "PUT" => 201 _ => 400 }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Guards
docs.body: Guards Without types With types match r { Result.Ok(v) if v > 0 => v Result.Ok(_v) => 0 Result.Err(e) => e } match r { Result.Ok(v) if v > 0 => v Result.Ok(_v) => 0 Result.Err(e) => e }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Payload enums
docs.body: Payload enums Import stdlib/option.ny for generic Option<T> / Result<T,E> , or declare a monomorph enum: Without types With types enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } let x = Option_i32.Some(42) let n = match x { Option_i32.Some(v) => v, Option_i32.None => 0, } enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } let x = Option_i32.Some(42) let n = match x { Option_i32.Some(v) => v, Option_i32.None => 0, } match on a parameter typed Option<T> or Result<T,E> lowers the same as on a local binding. Result.Ok(7) at a call site is monomorphized from the callee parameter type (no extra let annotation needed).

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: String matching
docs.body: String matching match works on string scrutinees with string literal arms (compared via strcmp ). Use _ for the default arm. Without types With types fn http_status(method) { return match method { "GET" => 200, "POST" => 201, _ => 404, } } fn http_status(method: i32) -> i32 { return match method { "GET" => 200, "POST" => 201, _ => 404, } } Arm bodies may use a trailing comma. Built-in tag-only Option / Result (no import) support ?? / ?. only — not stored payloads. Syntax →

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Memory & ownership
docs.body: Memory & ownership How Nyra manages memory at compile time — no garbage collector, no manual free in normal code, and no leaks when you follow the rules the compiler enforces. For developer-friendly ownership (auto-borrow, auto-Copy, inference) see Ownership UX . On this page Philosophy: safety without a GC Ownership UX Copy vs Move types Ownership rules Automatic cleanup (Drop plan) Non-lexical borrows (NLL) Lifetimes How Nyra prevents memory leaks Double-free & use-after-free defer (LIFO cleanup) Custom Drop for structs spawn, closures & Send/Sync FFI boundary Compiler pipeline Common errors & fixes Examples cookbook

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Philosophy: safety without a GC
docs.body: Philosophy: safety without a GC Nyra uses compile-time ownership inspired by Rust, with Go-like syntax. Every value has a clear owner. When ownership ends, the compiler inserts cleanup code — you never call free on ordinary strings or owned return values. No garbage collector — deterministic, low-latency native code. No reference counting in the language core — ownership is static. Zero cost for i32 , bool , enums without payloads — stack only. Compile errors instead of runtime crashes for use-after-move, double-free, and dangling references. Escape analysis — Nyra proves local values at compile time and promotes them to the stack; sequential channels can become lock-free LocalChannel . See Escape analysis .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Ownership UX
docs.body: Ownership UX Nyra keeps Rust’s static ownership model but reduces day-to-day friction: Auto-borrow — save(user) becomes save(&user) when the callee expects &User . Auto-Copy — structs like Point { x: i32 y: i32 } copy on assign without #[derive(Copy)] . Inference — return types, generic call sites ( id(7) ), and let price = 3.14 → f64 . Explicit control — move user / clone user at calls; Swift-style errors with fix-it notes. Full guide: Ownership UX · Conformance: CONF-INF-* , CONF-COERCE-* , CONF-DIAG-* , CONF-COPY-* , CONF-F64-* . Without types With types struct User { name: string age: i32 } fn save(u: &User) { print(u.name) } fn main() { let user = User { name: "Ada", age: 30 } save(user) print(user.name) } struct User { name: string age: i32 } fn save(u: &User) { print(u.name) } fn main() -> void { let user: User = User { name: "Ada", age: 30 } save(user) print(user.name) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Copy vs Move types
docs.body: Copy vs Move types The compiler classifies every type as Copy or Move ( ownership/kind.rs ). Kind Types On assignment / pass At scope end Copy i32 , i64 , u32 , f64 , bool , enum tags, ptr , fn pointers, all-Copy structs Bitwise copy — both names valid Stack discarded — no heap call Move string (heap C-string), structs with move fields, types with impl Drop Ownership transfers — old name invalid Auto free or custom Drop_*_drop Without types With types fn main() { let a = 10 let b = a // Copy — both usable print(a) print(b) let s = "hello" let t = s // Move — s is now invalid print(t) // print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' } fn main() -> void { let a: i32 = 10 let b = a // Copy — both usable print(a) print(b) let s: string = "hello" let t = s // Move — s is now invalid print(t) // print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Ownership rules
docs.body: Ownership rules One owner — each heap-owned value has exactly one binding responsible for cleanup. Move by default — passing a string to a function or assigning it moves ownership unless the binding is mut and the callee borrows. Borrow, don’t steal — &T and &mut T temporarily share access without transferring ownership. No active borrows on move — you cannot move a value while an immutable or mutable reference to it is live (NLL tracks this). Returned owned values — functions like read_file , strcat return new heap string s; the caller owns the result. Without types With types fn greet(name) { return strcat("Hello, ", name) } fn main() { let msg = greet("Nyra") print(msg) } fn greet(name: string) -> i32 { return strcat("Hello, ", name) } fn main() -> void { let msg = greet("Nyra") print(msg) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Automatic cleanup (Drop plan)
docs.body: Automatic cleanup (Drop plan) After type-checking, the ownership analyzer builds a DropPlan per function ( ownership/drop.rs ): Scan every let binding and infer whether it owns heap memory. Track moves — if a value is moved, mark it so it is not dropped twice. At each scope exit (end of block, before return , on branch merge), codegen emits emit_auto_drops . For string locals still owned → call free(ptr) . For structs with impl Drop → call Drop_TypeName_drop . Without types With types fn load_and_print() { let data = read_file("config.txt") // owned heap string print(data) } // compiler inserts: free(data) fn load_and_print() { let data = read_file("config.txt") // owned heap string print(data) } // compiler inserts: free(data) You do not write free(data) in normal Nyra code. The compiler guarantees cleanup on every path that still owns the binding.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Composite struct cleanup
docs.body: Composite struct cleanup Structs with heap-owned fields (e.g. string ) but no custom impl Drop get automatic field-wise cleanup — the compiler emits free for each owned field at scope end: Without types With types fn main() { let p = { id: 1, body: read_file("data.txt") } print(p.id) } // free(p.body) inserted automatically fn main() -> void { let p = { id: 1, body: read_file("data.txt") } print(p.id) } // free(p.body) inserted automatically Nested structs without custom Drop are dropped recursively. Custom impl Drop still takes full control (no double field-free).

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Owned extern fn returns
docs.body: Owned extern fn returns Any extern fn foo(...) -> string in your program is automatically treated as returning heap-owned memory — no need to register symbols in a compiler whitelist: Without types With types extern fn my_api_load(){ let s = my_api_load() // auto-owned, auto-dropped print(s) } extern fn my_api_load(){ let s = my_api_load() // auto-owned, auto-dropped print(s) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Partial field moves
docs.body: Partial field moves Moving a single field out of a struct invalidates the parent binding (Rust-style), preventing use-after-partial-move bugs: Without types With types fn main() { let p = { a: 1, body: "hello" } let body = p.body // move field — p is now invalid print(body) // print(p.a) // ERROR: use of moved value 'p' } fn main() -> void { let p = { a: 1, body: "hello" } let body = p.body // move field — p is now invalid print(body) // print(p.a) // ERROR: use of moved value 'p' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Generic Option / Result
docs.body: Generic Option / Result Without types With types enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) } let msg = Option.Some("hello") // monomorph → Option__string let n = Option.Some(42) // literal → Option__i32 enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) } let msg = Option.Some("hello") // monomorph → Option__string let n = Option.Some(42) // literal → Option__i32 Heap payloads in generic enums (e.g. Option<string> ) get automatic payload cleanup at scope end. Import from stdlib: import "stdlib/option.ny" . Typed annotations and match patterns rewrite to monomorphized names ( Option__string ) at compile time.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Monolith-friendly structs + strings
docs.body: Monolith-friendly structs + strings Large single-file apps can combine multi-field structs, partial field moves, generic Option<string> , and Box<T> without manual Drop for string fields: Without types With types import "stdlib/option.ny" import "stdlib/box.ny" struct UserRecord Send { id: i32, name: string, email: string } fn lookup(id, label) { if id > 0 { return Option.Some(label) } return Option.None } fn main() { let u = UserRecord { id: 1, name: "Ada", email: "ada@nyra.dev" } let id = u.id let name = u.name let opt = Option.Some(name) match opt { Option.Some(n) => print(n), Option.None => print("none"), } let boxed = Box<string> { value: "cache" } print(id) print(boxed.value) } import "stdlib/option.ny" import "stdlib/box.ny" struct UserRecord Send { id: i32, name: string, email: string } fn lookup(id: i32, label: i32) -> i32 { if id > 0 { return Option.Some(label) } return Option.None } fn main() -> void { let u: UserRecord = UserRecord { id: 1, name: "Ada", email: "ada@nyra.dev" } let id = u.id let name = u.name let opt = Option.Some(name) match opt { Option.Some(n) => print(n), Option.None => print("none"), } let boxed = Box<string> { value: "cache" } print(id) print(boxed.value) } See examples/monolith_struct_smoke.ny for a full compile-ready sample.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Shared ownership with Arc<T>
docs.body: Shared ownership with Arc<T> Reference-counted sharing for graphs and cross-scope labels. Import stdlib/arc.ny : Without types With types import "stdlib/arc.ny" struct GraphEdge Send { from_id: i32 to_id: i32 shared_label: Arc<string> } fn share_label(name) { let root = Arc_from_string(name) return Arc_clone_string(root) } fn main() { let shared = share_label("node-alpha") let edge = GraphEdge { from_id: 1, to_id: 2, shared_label: shared } print(Arc_get_string(edge.shared_label)) } import "stdlib/arc.ny" struct GraphEdge Send { from_id: i32 to_id: i32 shared_label: Arc<string> } fn share_label(name: string) -> i32 { let root = Arc_from_string(name) return Arc_clone_string(root) } fn main() -> void { let shared = share_label("node-alpha") let edge: GraphEdge = GraphEdge { from_id: 1, to_id: 2, shared_label: shared } print(Arc_get_string(edge.shared_label)) } Arc<T> is Send + Sync ; Arc_clone_string / Arc_clone_i32 increment refcount; last Drop frees the payload ( arc_dec_i32 / arc_dec_string ). See examples/graph_arc_smoke.ny .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: When auto-drop runs
docs.body: When auto-drop runs Event What gets dropped End of fn / block All owned bindings still in scope and not moved return Owned locals not returned as the value Move to function arg Source binding — callee or copy owns cleanup defer expr Deferred expressions run LIFO before scope exit

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Non-lexical borrows (NLL)
docs.body: Non-lexical borrows (NLL) Unlike lexical-only systems, Nyra ends borrows at the last use of the reference, not at the closing brace. This is Non-Lexical Lifetimes ( ownership/nll.rs → compute_last_uses ). Without types With types fn main() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &s // immutable borrow starts print(r) // last use of r — borrow ENDS here s = "world" // OK — no active borrow } fn main() -> void { let mut s = "hello" let r = &s // immutable borrow starts print(r) // last use of r — borrow ENDS here s = "world" // OK — no active borrow } Without types With types fn broken() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &mut s // mutable borrow print(s) // ERROR: cannot use s while mutably borrowed print(r) } fn broken() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &mut s // mutable borrow print(s) // ERROR: cannot use s while mutably borrowed print(r) } See also: Nyra Borrowing for a guided lesson.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Lifetimes
docs.body: Lifetimes References carry lifetimes so the compiler can reject dangling references (returning &local after the local is destroyed). Without types With types // ERROR: cannot return reference to local fn dangling(){ let s = "tmp" return &s } // OK: return value transfers ownership instead fn owned() { let s = "tmp" return s } // ERROR: cannot return reference to local fn dangling(){ let s: string = "tmp" return &s } // OK: return value transfers ownership instead fn owned() -> i32 { let s: string = "tmp" return s } Explicit: fn pick<'a>(x: &'a string) -> &'a string Elision: one input reference → output shares that lifetime HRTB: for<'a> fn(&'a string) -> &'a string for higher-order callbacks

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: How Nyra prevents memory leaks
docs.body: How Nyra prevents memory leaks A memory leak is heap memory that is never freed. Nyra prevents leaks in normal code through:

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 1. Drop plan covers every owned binding
docs.body: 1. Drop plan covers every owned binding If you write let s = read_file(...) and never move s , the compiler always emits free at scope end — even if you forget the variable exists.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 2. Moves transfer cleanup responsibility
docs.body: 2. Moves transfer cleanup responsibility Without types With types fn consume(s) { print(s) } // consume's drop plan frees s here fn main() { let path = "data.txt" let content = read_file(path) consume(content) // ownership moved to consume — main won't double-free } fn consume(s: string) { print(s) } // consume's drop plan frees s here fn main() -> void { let path: string = "data.txt" let content = read_file(path) consume(content) // ownership moved to consume — main won't double-free }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 3. No silent heap allocation without ownership
docs.body: 3. No silent heap allocation without ownership Functions that return heap memory ( strcat , read_file , sys_recv , …) are registered in OWNED_EXTERN_RETURNS . The caller must bind the result — orphaning is impossible because unused expressions in let still get a binding or the value is moved/consumed.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 4. Closure & spawn heap env freed at scope end
docs.body: 4. Closure & spawn heap env freed at scope end Escaping closures that malloc capture envs register heap_owned metadata — freed when the let binding ends. Stack closures need no heap free.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 5. Custom Drop for resource types
docs.body: 5. Custom Drop for resource types Without types With types struct FileHandle { fd: i32 } impl Drop for FileHandle { fn drop(self) { nyra_close(self.fd) // always runs when FileHandle goes out of scope } } struct FileHandle { fd: i32 } impl Drop for FileHandle { fn drop(self) { nyra_close(self.fd) // always runs when FileHandle goes out of scope } }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: What Nyra does not prevent automatically
docs.body: What Nyra does not prevent automatically Intentional leak via FFI — returning a raw ptr to C without documenting ownership. Manual free on live bindings — compiler warns; double-free if you free then auto-drop runs. Cycles — no cycle collector; avoid reference cycles in raw pointers.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Double-free & use-after-free
docs.body: Double-free & use-after-free Nyra blocks these at compile time: Bug Compile-time check Example error Use after move Move tracking in borrowck Use of moved value 's' Double free Moved values skipped in DropPlan Cannot happen if code compiles Free then use free on owned binding → warning + moved mark Manual free warning Dangling & Lifetime checker cannot return reference to local Data race (thread) Send / Sync on spawn captures captured value is not Send Without types With types fn main() { let s = "hello" let t = s // move print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' } fn main() -> void { let s: string = "hello" let t = s // move print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: defer vs Drop (Extended)
docs.body: defer vs Drop (Extended) Name: defer expr Explanation: Schedules a call at scope exit (LIFO). Prefer impl Drop for resources — do not defer free on owned strings. Example: methods gallery · nyra run examples/builtins/defer/defer_return.ny Without types With types allow_extended fn cleanup() { print(1) } fn main() { defer cleanup() return } allow_extended fn cleanup() { print(1) } fn main() -> void { defer cleanup() return } Output: 1 (before function returns). LIFO demo: #ex-defer-lifo → body , a , b . defer schedules a call expression at scope exit (LIFO). It lives in the Extended tier. For most code, auto-drop and impl Drop replace defer — do not use defer free on owned strings (double-free risk). Need Prefer defer ? Heap string at } Auto-drop No FFI handle / file impl Drop wrapper (RAII) Only one-off defer extern_close(h) if you skip a struct Log at scope exit Write before return or in Drop Optional defer log_done() Without types With types extern fn gzclose(f){ handle: ptr } impl Drop for GzFile { fn drop(self) { unsafe { gzclose(self.handle) } } } fn main() { let f = GzFile { handle: open_gz("log.txt.gz") } print(read_all(f.handle)) } // gzclose via Drop — preferred over defer extern fn gzclose(f){ handle: ptr } impl Drop for GzFile { fn drop(self) { unsafe { gzclose(self.handle) } } } fn main() -> void { let f = GzFile { handle: open_gz("log.txt.gz") } print(read_all(f.handle)) } // gzclose via Drop — preferred over defer Why Extended, not Core: Drop covers cleanup semantics Core needs; defer overlaps and is mainly for niche FFI/side-effect hooks. Core-only CI uses nyra check --deny-extended with auto-drop only. See nyra-skill.md § defer vs Drop.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Custom Drop for structs
docs.body: Custom Drop for structs Without types With types struct Buffer { data: ptr, len: i32 } impl Drop for Buffer { fn drop(self) { free(self.data) } } fn main() { let b = Buffer { data: allocate(), len: 1024 } } // Drop_Buffer_drop called automatically struct Buffer { data: ptr, len: i32 } impl Drop for Buffer { fn drop(self) { free(self.data) } } fn main() -> void { let b: Buffer = Buffer { data: allocate(), len: 1024 } } // Drop_Buffer_drop called automatically Structs with impl Drop are always Move types — they cannot be silently copied.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: spawn, closures & Send/Sync
docs.body: spawn, closures & Send/Sync

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: spawn
docs.body: spawn spawn { ... } packs captured variables into a struct, heap-copies it, and starts a pthread . Rules: Copy captures ( i32 , bool ) — copied into the thread struct. Move captures ( string ) — must be Send ; marked moved in the parent. Active & / &mut — compile error.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Closures
docs.body: Closures Capturing lambdas follow the same rules — no reference captures; move types transfer ownership. See Closures .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: FFI boundary
docs.body: FFI boundary Inside Nyra: auto-drop. At the C boundary: export fn returning string → caller outside Nyra must call free . extern fn returning string → Nyra caller auto-owns and auto-drops. Do not call free on live Nyra bindings — the compiler warns. Full ABI: FFI & ABI .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Compiler pipeline
docs.body: Compiler pipeline Source (.ny) → Lexer → Parser → Macro expand (??, ?., struct ctors, move/clone desugar) → Monomorph (+ generic call-site inference) → Auto-borrow coercion → Const fold → Typecheck → Ownership analysis → DropPlan (+ auto-Copy inference) → Borrow check (NLL moves/borrows, callee-aware diagnostics) → Lifetime check → Send / Sync check → LLVM codegen (auto-drops, spawn, closures, f64/double) → opt → clang + Nyra runtime → binary Inspect without codegen: nyra check . · Structured errors: nyra diag . --json · Ownership snapshot: nyra inspect name --at main.ny:42 · Ownership inspect guide

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Common errors & fixes
docs.body: Common errors & fixes Error Cause Fix Use of moved value 'x' Used after move Borrow with & , use clone x / .clone() , or change callee to &T (auto-borrow applies) Cannot borrow as mutable Two &mut or & + &mut Shrink borrow scope; use NLL — end borrow before second use cannot return reference to local Dangling ref Return owned string or take &'a parameter cannot capture reference in closure & in lambda capture Capture owned copy or use Send move value free on live owned binding Manual free + auto-drop Remove manual free; let auto-drop handle it More: Diagnostics

docs.heading: Examples cookbook
docs.body: Examples cookbook

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Read file — zero manual free
docs.body: Read file — zero manual free Without types With types extern fn read_file(path){ let text = read_file("README.md") print(text) } // free(text) inserted by compiler extern fn read_file(path){ let text = read_file("README.md") print(text) } // free(text) inserted by compiler

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Concatenate strings — both operands moved unless borrowed
docs.body: Concatenate strings — both operands moved unless borrowed Without types With types extern fn strcat(a, b){ let a = "Hello, " let b = "Nyra" let msg = strcat(a, b) // a and b moved into strcat; new string returned print(msg) } // only msg freed — a,b already consumed extern fn strcat(a, b){ let a: string = "Hello, " let b: string = "Nyra" let msg = strcat(a, b) // a and b moved into strcat; new string returned print(msg) } // only msg freed — a,b already consumed

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Safe mutation with &mut
docs.body: Safe mutation with &mut Without types With types fn bump(mut n) { n = n + 1 print(n) } fn main() { let mut count = 0 bump(count) // i32 is Copy — count still valid print(count) } fn bump(mut n: i32) { n = n + 1 print(n) } fn main() -> void { let mut count: i32 = 0 bump(count) // i32 is Copy — count still valid print(count) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Option ?? — no leak on either branch
docs.body: Option ?? — no leak on either branch Without types With types import "stdlib/option.ny" fn main() { let x = Option.None let y = x ?? 42 // i32 Copy — no heap print(y) } import "stdlib/option.ny" fn main() -> void { let x = Option.None let y = x ?? 42 // i32 Copy — no heap print(y) } ← Nyra Borrowing Learn overview Concurrency → Language reference →

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: String methods no import
docs.body: String methods no import Method syntax on string — borrows the receiver (does not move). Heap copy: clone s keyword. Method Args Returns Description Example .length() / .len() — i32 Byte length "hello".len() .split(sep) string split list Split on separator "a,b,c".split(",") .trim() — string Strip whitespace " hi ".trim() .contains(needle) string i32 1 if found "abc".contains("b") .starts_with(prefix) string i32 Prefix test "hello".starts_with("he") .ends_with(suffix) string i32 Suffix test "hello".ends_with("lo") .replace(from, to) 2 × string string Replace all "a-b".replace("-", "_") .replacen(from, to, count) 2 × string , i32 string Replace at most count "aaa".replacen("a", "b", 2) .to_upper() — string ASCII upper-case "hi".to_upper() .to_lower() — string ASCII lower-case "HI".to_lower() clone s — string Heap copy (keyword) let copy = clone s .strip_prefix(prefix) string string Remove prefix if present "pre_hi".strip_prefix("pre_") .strip_suffix(suffix) string string Remove suffix if present "hi.txt".strip_suffix(".txt") .index(needle) string i32 First byte index or -1 "hello".index("ell") .last_index(needle) string i32 Last occurrence index "aba".last_index("a") .is_empty() — i32 1 if length 0 "".is_empty() .count(needle) string i32 Non-overlapping matches "hello".count("l") .repeat(n) i32 string Repeat n times "ab".repeat(3) .trim_start() / .trim_end() — string Strip leading / trailing whitespace " hi".trim_start() .splitn(sep, n) string , i32 VecStr Split into at most n parts "a,b,c".splitn(",", 2) .split_once(sep) string string Part before first separator "a:b:c".split_once(":") .fields() — VecStr Whitespace-split words "a b c".fields() .pad_start(w, pad) / .pad_end(w, pad) i32 , string string Pad to width "hi".pad_start(5, "0") .to_snake_case() … .to_pascal_case() — string Case conversion helpers "Hello World".to_snake_case() .compare(other) / .equal_fold(other) string i32 Lexicographic / case-insensitive compare "abc".compare("abd") .index_byte(b) / .last_index_byte(b) i32 i32 Byte search; -1 if missing "abc".index_byte(97) .substring(start, len) 2 × i32 string Slice by byte offset + length "hello".substring(1, 3) .push_char(ch) / .pop() i32 / — string Append byte / remove last byte "ab".push_char(99) .after_sep(sep) / .before_sep(sep) string string Part after / before first separator "a:b".after_sep(":") .strip_ansi() — string Remove ANSI escape codes s.strip_ansi() .is_ascii() — i32 1 if all bytes ≤ 127 "hello".is_ascii() .collapse_ws() — string Collapse runs of whitespace to single space " a b ".collapse_ws() .reverse() — string Reverse byte order (new string) "abc".reverse() .is_digit() / .is_alpha() / .is_alnum() — i32 ASCII digit / letter / alphanumeric tests "123".is_digit() .common_prefix_len(other) string i32 Shared prefix length in bytes "abc".common_prefix_len("abx") .pad_center(w, pad) i32 , string string Center-pad to width "hi".pad_center(6, " ") .escape_json() / .truncate(n) / .split_after(sep) varies string JSON escape / byte cap / split after separator "a\\"b".escape_json() Examples: .split() · .trim() · .strip_prefix() · .fields() · for c in string

Method / ctor Args Returns Notes Example vec() — VecI32 Empty vector let v = vec() .push(x) i32 VecI32 Append (chains) v.push(42) .get(i) / .set(i, v) index, value i32 / self Indexed access v.get(0) .len() / .pop() — i32 Length / pop last v.len() .contains(x) / .includes(x) i32 i32 1 / 0 v.contains(42) .find(pred, fb) / .find_eq(x, fb) pred / value i32 Search with fallback v.find_eq(10, -1) .filter(pred) / .map(f) / .reduce(init, f) fn VecI32 / value HOFs v.filter(is_even) vec_range(start, end) 2 × i32 VecI32 Half-open range vec_range(0, 10) .insert(i, x) i32 , i32 VecI32 Insert at index v.insert(0, 9) .remove(i) i32 VecI32 Remove at index (chains) v.remove(0) .clear() — VecI32 Remove all elements v.clear() .reverse() — VecI32 Reverse in place v.reverse() .sort() — VecI32 Sort ascending in place v.sort() .binary_search(x) i32 i32 Index on sorted vec or -1 v.binary_search(5) .swap(i, j) / .extend(other) / .append(x) indices / VecI32 / i32 VecI32 Swap elements / merge vectors v.extend(other) .capacity() / .reserve(n) / .fill(x) i32 i32 / self Capacity / preallocate / fill in place v.reserve(64) .swap_remove(i) / .is_empty() i32 / — i32 / i32 O(1) remove (unordered) / empty test v.swap_remove(1) .slice(start, end) / .window(start, size) / .retain(pred) i32 / fn VecI32 Sub-range copy / in-place filter v.window(0, 3) Example: vec() . docs.heading: Other builtins no import
docs.body: Other builtins no import Call Returns / effect Notes Example random() / random(min, max) generic integer ChaCha20 CSPRNG; return type follows bounds ( i32 , i64 , u64 , …). No-arg defaults to i32 . random(1, 6) · random<i64>(50, 100) random_f64() / random_f64(min, max) f64 Unit interval [0, 1) or range [min, max) random_f64(0.0, 1.0) date() Date struct Local clock fields let d = date(); print(d.year) spawn { ... } / spawn:task { ... } JoinHandle (expr) / none (stmt) Lightweight task pool; needs allow_extended ; not on wasm32 spawn { print(1) } spawn:thread { ... } JoinHandle (expr) Dedicated OS thread (~MB stack) spawn:thread { heavy() } h.join() void On JoinHandle ; blocks until spawn completes; consumes handle let h = spawn { … }; h.join() parallel for i in 0..n { ... } / parallel:task parallel loop Task pool (default); max = N , cpu = N% parallel for · parallel:task(max = 4) · cpu = N% · mode = … parallel:thread(...) for ... parallel loop OS thread fork-join per chunk parallel:thread(max = 4) progress for x in items { ... } loop + progress bar Optional label progress for defer expr scope-exit call (LIFO) Extended — prefer impl Drop defer · LIFO async fn … { … } promise / future handle Body on spawn:thread ; not on wasm32 async fn await handle blocks until complete Inside async fn or at call site await Examples: allow_extended · JoinHandle · random · date() · spawn · .join() · parallel for · progress · benchmark · defer · async/await

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: File directive: allow_extended
docs.body: File directive: allow_extended spawn , parallel for , async / await , defer , and related APIs live in Nyra’s Stable Extended tier. Put this line at the top of the file (before fn main or imports) when the unit intentionally uses those features: allow_extended What it does Why use it Marks the file as an Extended-tier program Documents intent for readers and reviewers Suppresses warning[W001] Extended-feature warnings are silenced in this file (useful when CI runs with stricter flags) One line per file Not per-function — applies to the whole compilation unit You do not need allow_extended for Core-only code (plain print , structs, loops, etc.). All spawn and parallel examples below include it. See also Concurrency → spawn and Roadmap (Core vs Extended). Name: allow_extended (first line of file) Explanation: Declares that this compilation unit intentionally uses Stable Extended APIs. Does not enable features by itself — it documents intent and integrates with stability warnings. Example: Without types With types allow_extended fn main() { parallel for i in 0..3 { print(i) } } allow_extended fn main() -> void { parallel for i in 0..3 { print(i) } } Output: Same as parallel for (iteration order non-deterministic). Without this line, Extended syntax still compiles in default builds; the directive is recommended in files that use spawn , parallel for , etc. Platform: spawn is unavailable on wasm32-wasi (compile error). Native Linux, macOS, and Windows only.

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: JoinHandle and .join()
docs.body: JoinHandle and .join() When spawn appears as an expression ( let h = spawn { … } ), the type of h is JoinHandle — an opaque handle to a background task or thread. It is not a numeric ID; you cannot print it or compare it. You either wait on it or drop it. Method Signature Effect .join() h.join() -> void Blocks the current thread until the spawned work finishes. Consumes the handle (move) — you cannot call .join() twice on the same variable. Form Syntax Wait for completion? Statement spawn { … } No — fire-and-forget; runtime detaches immediately Expression + join let h = spawn { … }; h.join() Yes — main waits at h.join() Drop without join let h = spawn { … } then scope ends No — handle dropped → detach (same as statement form) .join() takes no arguments . Works the same for spawn , spawn:task , and spawn:thread ; the compiler calls the correct runtime ( spawn_task_join vs spawn_join ) based on how the handle was created. See example .

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: Array_push / map / filter / reduce
docs.body: Array_push / map / filter / reduce Without types With types import "stdlib/builtins_array.ny" import "stdlib/vec.ny" fn is_even(x){ if x % 2 == 0 { return 1 } return 0 } fn mul10(x){ return x * 10 } fn sum(acc, x){ return acc + x } fn main() { let v = Vec_i32_new() Array_push(v, 1) Array_push(v, 2) Array_push(v, 3) Array_push(v, 4) let mapped = Array_map(v, mul10) let evens = Array_filter(v, is_even) let total = Array_reduce(v, 0, sum) let found = Array_find(v, is_even, -1) print(Vec_i32_len(mapped)) print(Vec_i32_len(evens)) print(total) print(found) print(Array_pop(v)) } import "stdlib/builtins_array.ny" import "stdlib/vec.ny" fn is_even(x: i32){ if x % 2 == 0 { return 1 } return 0 } fn mul10(x: i32){ return x * 10 } fn sum(acc: i32, x: i32){ return acc + x } fn main() -> void { let v: ptr = Vec_i32_new() Array_push(v, 1) Array_push(v, 2) Array_push(v, 3) Array_push(v, 4) let mapped: ptr = Array_map(v, mul10) let evens: ptr = Array_filter(v, is_even) let total: i32 = Array_reduce(v, 0, sum) let found = Array_find(v, is_even, -1) print(Vec_i32_len(mapped)) print(Vec_i32_len(evens)) print(total) print(found) print(Array_pop(v)) } Output 4 2 10 2 4

docs.heading: vec() filter/map/reduce/contains
docs.body: vec() filter/map/reduce/contains Without types With types fn is_even(x){ if x % 2 == 0 { return 1 } return 0 } fn times2(x){ return x * 2 } fn add(a, b){ return a + b } fn main() { let xs = vec().push(1).push(2).push(3).push(4) print(xs.len()) print(xs.contains(3)) print(xs.filter(is_even).len()) print(xs.map(times2).get(0)) print(xs.reduce(0, add)) print(vec_range(0, 3).len()) } fn is_even(x: i32){ if x % 2 == 0 { return 1 } return 0 } fn times2(x: i32){ return x * 2 } fn add(a: i32, b: i32){ return a + b } fn main() -> void { let xs = vec().push(1).push(2).push(3).push(4) print(xs.len()) print(xs.contains(3)) print(xs.filter(is_even).len()) print(xs.map(times2).get(0)) print(xs.reduce(0, add)) print(vec_range(0, 3).len()) } Output 4 1 2 2 10 3

docs.body: async_future_string Without types With types // Future<string> — typed async with Executor_sleep_ms. allow_extended import "stdlib/async/future.ny" async fn greet(){ let h = Executor_sleep_ms(10) await h return "Nyra async v2" } fn main() { let f = greet() print(await f) } // Future<string> — typed async with Executor_sleep_ms. allow_extended import "stdlib/async/future.ny" async fn greet(){ let h = Executor_sleep_ms(10) await h return "Nyra async v2" } fn main() -> void { let f = greet() print(await f) } Output Nyra async v2

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: async_spawn
docs.body: async_spawn Without types With types // async fn + await — call site gets handle immediately; body runs on spawn:thread. allow_extended import "stdlib/async_v1.ny" async fn compute(){ return 42 } fn main() { let h = compute() print(await h) } // async fn + await — call site gets handle immediately; body runs on spawn:thread. allow_extended import "stdlib/async_v1.ny" async fn compute(){ return 42 } fn main() -> void { let h = compute() print(await h) } Output 42 note: prebuilt runtime missing required symbols; compiling rt modules

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: async_state_machine
docs.body: async_state_machine Without types With types // async state machine — multiple await in one async fn (cooperative poll loop). allow_extended import "stdlib/async_v1.ny" async fn chain(){ let _ = await Executor_sleep_ms(5) let _ = await Executor_sleep_ms(5) return 100 } fn main() { let h = chain() print(await h) } // async state machine — multiple await in one async fn (cooperative poll loop). allow_extended import "stdlib/async_v1.ny" async fn chain(){ let _ = await Executor_sleep_ms(5) let _ = await Executor_sleep_ms(5) return 100 } fn main() -> void { let h = chain() print(await h) } Output 100
